name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
عَمَّ يَتَسَآءَلُونَ (١)
```

```english
What is it that they are questioning each other about?! (1)
```

```arabic
عَنِ ٱلنَّبَإِ ٱلْعَظِيمِ (٢)
```

```english
[Is it] about the Great Tiding, (2)
```

```arabic
ٱلَّذِى هُمْ فِيهِ مُخْتَلِفُونَ (٣)
```

```english
the one about which they differ? (3)
```

```arabic
كَلَّا سَيَعْلَمُونَ (٤)
```

```english
No indeed! Soon they will know! (4)
```

```arabic
ثُمَّ كَلَّا سَيَعْلَمُونَ (٥)
```

```english
No indeed! Soon they will know for once again! (5)
```

```arabic
أَلَمْ نَجْعَلِ ٱلْأَرْضَ مِهَـٰدًا (٦)
```

```english
Did We not make the earth a resting place? (6)
```

```arabic
وَٱلْجِبَالَ أَوْتَادًا (٧)
```

```english
and the mountains stakes? (7)
```

```arabic
وَخَلَقْنَـٰكُمْ أَزْوَٰجًا (٨)
```

```english
and create you in pairs? (8)
```

```arabic
وَجَعَلْنَا نَوْمَكُمْ سُبَاتًا (٩)
```

```english
and make your sleep for rest? (9)
```

```arabic
وَجَعَلْنَا ٱلَّيْلَ لِبَاسًا (١٠)
```

```english
and make the night a covering? (10)
```

```arabic
وَجَعَلْنَا ٱلنَّهَارَ مَعَاشًا (١١)
```

```english
and make the day for livelihood? (11)
```

```arabic
وَبَنَيْنَا فَوْقَكُمْ سَبْعًا شِدَادًا (١٢)
```

```english
and build above you the seven mighty heavens? (12)
```

```arabic
وَجَعَلْنَا سِرَاجًا وَهَّاجًا (١٣)
```

```english
and make [the sun for] a radiant lamp? (13)
```

```arabic
وَأَنزَلْنَا مِنَ ٱلْمُعْصِرَٰتِ مَآءً ثَجَّاجًا (١٤)
```

```english
and send down water pouring from the rain-clouds, (14)
```

```arabic
لِّنُخْرِجَ بِهِۦ حَبًّا وَنَبَاتًا (١٥)
```

```english
that We may bring forth with it grains and plants, (15)
```

```arabic
وَجَنَّـٰتٍ أَلْفَافًا (١٦)
```

```english
and luxuriant gardens? (16)
```

## (Commentary)

The Surah encompasses the announcement of the coming of the Day of Decision (يوم الفصل) and its description, as well as the argumentation that it is a truth in which there is no doubt. It opens by mentioning their questioning regarding its news, followed by a response in a tone of threat (التهديد) stating that they shall soon know. It then argues for its reality by pointing to the witnessed order (النظام المشهود) in the cosmos, with its wise governance (التدبير الحكيم) which indicates with the clearest evidence that behind this changing and revolving life (النشأة), there is a stable and eternal life. It further indicates that following this abode, in which there is action (عمل) but no recompense (جزاء), there is an abode in which there is recompense but no action. Thus, there is a Day that this cosmic order articulates.

Then, it describes that Day in terms of what occurs therein: the summoning of the people, their presence, the turning of the rebellious (الطاغين) toward a painful punishment, and the God-conscious (المتقين) toward an enduring bliss. The discourse concludes with a word of warning (الإنذار). The Surah is Meccan, as evidenced by the context (سياق) of its verses.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **عَمَّ يَتَساءَلُونَ** — **"About what are they questioning one another?"** (78:1). The word **عَمَّ** is originally **عَمَّا**; the *alif* is consistently omitted from the interrogative **ما** when it is preceded by a preposition, such as **لِمَ** (for what), **مِمَّ** (from what), **عَلامَ** (upon what), and **إِلامَ** (until what). Questioning one another (التساؤل) refers to a group of people asking each other about a matter, or asking one after another about a matter even if the one being questioned is someone else. Thus, they were either asking one another about a matter, or they were asking the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله one after another about a matter. Since the context of the Surah is that of a response dominated by warning and threat, it supports the view that the questioners were the disbelievers (كفار) of Mecca from among the polytheists (المشركين) who denied prophethood (النبوة) and the Return (المعاد), rather than the believers or both groups together.

Thus, the questioning was from the polytheists, and reporting it in the form of an interrogation is to signal its insignificance and triviality, as the answer to it is so manifest that they ought not to have questioned it.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **عَنِ النَّبَإِ الْعَظِيمِ الَّذِي هُمْ فِيهِ مُخْتَلِفُونَ** — **"About the great tiding, (2) concerning which they differ."** (78:2-3). This is the answer to the preceding interrogation; that is, they are questioning one another about the great tiding (النبأ العظيم). The description of the tiding being questioned as "great" (العظيم) clearly serves to magnify it and emphasize its gravity.

What is meant by the "great tiding" is the news of the Resurrection (البعث) and the Day of Judgment (القيامة), which the Great Quran emphasizes with utmost concern in its Meccan surahs, especially in the early revelations (العتائق) sent down at the beginning of the prophetic mission.

This is supported by the context of the Surah's verses, which limit themselves to mentioning the description of the Day of Decision (يوم الفصل) and the preceding argument that it is a realized truth.

It has been said that what is meant is the news of the Great Quran, but this is refuted by the fact that the context, in terms of its primary focus, is foreign to it, even if the discourse is not devoid of an implicit reference to it.

It has also been said that the "great tiding" refers to everything they differed about, such as the affirmation of the Creator (الصانع) and His attributes, the angels, the messengers, the Resurrection, Paradise, Hell, and other matters. The proponent of this view considers the Surah's references to the truth of all these matters, which are encompassed by the true Islamic call (الدعوة).

This is refuted by the fact that the reference to all of these is a necessary concomitant of the description of the Resurrection, which includes the recompense for true belief, righteous deeds (العمل الصالح), disbelief, and criminality. These matters enter into the Surah's description of the Day of Decision as a consequence and by secondary intent (بالقصد الثاني).

Furthermore, the ones intended by these questioners—as previously mentioned—are the polytheists, and they affirmed the Creator and the angels while denying what lies beyond that among the mentioned matters.

His saying: **الَّذِي هُمْ فِيهِ مُخْتَلِفُونَ** — **"concerning which they differ"** (78:3). They only differed in the manner of their denial, while they were united in their rejection of it.

Some of them deemed it impossible and thus denied it, as is apparent in their saying, as recounted by Allah: **هَلْ نَدُلُّكُمْ عَلى رَجُلٍ يُنَبِّئُكُمْ إِذا مُزِّقْتُمْ كُلَّ مُمَزَّقٍ إِنَّكُمْ لَفِي خَلْقٍ جَدِيدٍ** — **"Shall we show you a man who informs you that when you have been totally torn apart, you will indeed be [re-created] in a new creation?"** (34:7). Others considered it far-fetched and thus denied it, which is their saying: **أَيَعِدُكُمْ أَنَّكُمْ إِذا مِتُّمْ وَكُنْتُمْ تُراباً وَعِظاماً أَنَّكُمْ مُخْرَجُونَ هَيْهاتَ هَيْهاتَ لِما تُوعَدُونَ** — **"Does he promise you that when you have died and become dust and bones you will be brought forth [from the graves]? (35) Far-fetched, far-fetched is what you are promised!"** (23:35-36). Others were in doubt regarding it and thus denied it; the Exalted said: **بَلِ ادَّارَكَ عِلْمُهُمْ فِي الْآخِرَةِ بَلْ هُمْ فِي شَكٍّ مِنْها** — **"Do they have a full knowledge of the Hereafter? No, they are in doubt about it."** (27:66). Still others were certain of it but did not believe out of obstinacy (عناداً), thus denying it just as they did not believe in monotheism (التوحيد), prophethood, and other branches of the religion after the proof was complete, out of sheer obstinacy; the Exalted said: **بَلْ لَجُّوا فِي عُتُوٍّ وَنُفُورٍ** — **"Rather they persist in rebellion and aversion."** (67:21).

The summary of the context of these three verses and what follows is that when they heard what the Quran warned them of regarding the Resurrection and the recompense on the Day of Decision, it weighed heavily upon them. They began asking one another about the nature of this strange news, the likes of which had not struck their ears until that day. Perhaps they also approached the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله and the believers, asking them about the description of that Day and saying, "When is this promise, if you are truthful?" They might have also consulted the People of the Book (أهل الكتاب), especially the Jews, regarding some of the Quranic truths that reached them and were contained in its new call, seeking their help in understanding it.

Allah has referred in this Surah to the story of their questioning in the form of a question and answer. He said: **عَمَّ يَتَساءَلُونَ** — which is a question about what they are asking. Then He said: **عَنِ النَّبَإِ الْعَظِيمِ الَّذِي هُمْ فِيهِ مُخْتَلِفُونَ** — which is the answer to the question of what they are asking. Then He said: **كَلَّا سَيَعْلَمُونَ** etc., which is the response to their questioning.

The commentators have many views regarding the vocabulary of these three verses and the presentation of their meanings, which we have omitted due to their lack of harmony with the context. What we have presented is what the context provides.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **كَلَّا سَيَعْلَمُونَ ثُمَّ كَلَّا سَيَعْلَمُونَ** — **"No indeed! Soon they will know! (4) Again, no indeed! Soon they will know!"** (78:4-5). This is a rebuff (ردع) of their questioning, based on their differing in denial. That is, let them be deterred from questioning, for the matter will soon be disclosed to them by the occurrence of this tiding, and they shall know it. In this expression, there is a threat (تهديد), as in His saying: **وَسَيَعْلَمُ الَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا أَيَّ مُنْقَلَبٍ يَنْقَلِبُونَ** — **"And the wrongdoers will soon know at what goal they will end up."** (26:227).

His saying: **ثُمَّ كَلَّا سَيَعْلَمُونَ** — **"Again, no indeed! Soon they will know!"** (78:5) is a reinforcement of the previous rebuff and threat. The tone of the threat is the contextual clue (القرينة) that the questioners are the polytheists who deny the Resurrection and recompense, rather than the believers or both groups together.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **أَلَمْ نَجْعَلِ الْأَرْضَ مِهاداً** — **"Did We not make the earth a resting place,"** (78:6). This verse, up to the completion of eleven verses, is presented as an argument (احتجاج) for the reality of the Resurrection and recompense, and the realization of this great tiding. A necessary consequence of its reality is the truth of what is in His saying: **سَيَعْلَمُونَ** — the announcement that they will witness it and thus know.

The presentation of the argument: The witnessed world (العالم المشهود) with its earth, sky, night, day, the procreating human race, the prevailing order therein, and the precise, masterful governance (التدبير المتقن) of its affairs—it is impossible for this to be a vain play (لعباً باطلاً) without a stable and eternal goal (غاية). It is therefore necessary that this transforming, changing, and revolving system be followed by a world possessing a stable and eternal order. It is also necessary that the effect of righteousness (الصلاح), toward which the human primordial nature (الفطرة) calls, and corruption (الفساد), from which it is deterred, should manifest therein. These effects—namely the happiness of the God-conscious and the misery of the corrupt—have not fully manifested in this witnessed world. It is impossible for Allah to instill in the primordial nature an instinctive call or an instinctive deterrent regarding something that has no trace in external reality and no share of occurrence. Thus, there is a Day that man shall meet, wherein he will be recompensed for his deeds: if good, then good; and if evil, then evil.

The verses are thus similar in meaning to His saying, the Exalted: **وَما خَلَقْنَا السَّماءَ وَالْأَرْضَ وَما بَيْنَهُما باطِلاً ذلِكَ ظَنُّ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا فَوَيْلٌ لِلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا مِنَ النَّارِ أَمْ نَجْعَلُ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ كَالْمُفْسِدِينَ فِي الْأَرْضِ أَمْ نَجْعَلُ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ كَالْمُفْسِدِينَ فِي الْأَرْضِ أَمْ نَجْعَلُ الْمُتَّقِينَ كَالْفُجَّارِ** — **"We did not create the sky and the earth and whatever is between them in vain. That is the opinion of those who are faithless. So woe to the faithless from the Fire! (27) Shall We treat those who have faith and do righteous deeds like those who cause corruption on the earth? Shall We treat the God-conscious like the vicious?"** (38:27-28).

By this explanation, it is established that there is a Day man shall meet and be recompensed for what he has done: if good, then good; and if evil, then evil. Thus, it is not for the polytheists to differ concerning it, such that some doubt it, a group considers it far-fetched, a people deem it impossible, and others—despite knowing it—do not believe out of obstinacy. The Day is necessary in its occurrence, and the recompense is beyond doubt.

It appears from some commentators that the verses are presented to prove Power (القدرة), and that the return (العود) is similar to the beginning (البدء), and He who is capable of initiating is capable of repeating. While this argument is sound and has occurred in His speech, it is an argument for possibility (الإمكان) rather than occurrence (الوقوع). The context here is one of occurrence rather than possibility, so the previous presentation is more appropriate.

In any case, His saying: **أَلَمْ نَجْعَلِ الْأَرْضَ مِهاداً** — **"Did We not make the earth a resting place,"** (78:6). The interrogation is for affirmation (الإنكار). The word **مِهاد** (resting place) refers to a bed or a stable place where one manages their affairs; it is also applied to the carpet upon which one sits. The meaning is: We have made the earth a stable place for you to settle upon and manage your affairs.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَالْجِبالَ أَوْتاداً** — **"and the mountains as pegs?"** (78:7). The word **أَوْتاد** is the plural of **وتد** (peg), which is like a nail but thicker, as mentioned in *al-Majma'*. Perhaps counting the mountains as pegs is based on the fact that the majority of the earth's mountains are formed by volcanic activity through the splitting of the earth, from which molten terrestrial materials emerge and stand at the mouth of the split, accumulating like a peg fixed in the earth. Through them, the eruption of the volcano beneath is calmed, and the agitation and shaking (الميدان) of the earth are removed.

It has been said by some that the meaning of making the mountains as pegs is the regulation of the livelihood of the earth's inhabitants through the benefits deposited therein; had it not been for them, the earth would have swayed with them—meaning it would not have been prepared for their benefit. This, however, is a diversion of the word from its literal meaning without a compelling necessity.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَخَلَقْناكُمْ أَزْواجاً** — **"And We created you in pairs,"** (78:8). That is, pair by pair, male and female, so that the law of procreation (التناسل) may run among you, and the survival of the species may continue for as long as Allah wills.

It has been said that it refers to likenesses (الإشكال), meaning each of you is a counterpart to the other. It has also been said that it refers to types (الأصناف), meaning different types such as white, black, red, and yellow, etc. It has also been said that it refers to the creation of each of them from two fluids: the fluid of the man and the fluid of the woman. These are weak views.

It has been said that the shift (الالتفات) in the verse from the third person to the second person is for emphasis in binding the argument and rebuking (التبكيت).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَجَعَلْنا نَوْمَكُمْ سُباتاً** — **"and made your sleep for rest,"** (78:9). The word **سُبات** (rest) refers to comfort and tranquility, for in sleep there is stillness and rest for the physical animal faculties from the exhaustion and fatigue that befell them during wakefulness due to the soul's (النفس) utilization of them.

It has been said that **سُبات** means "cutting off" (القطع), and in sleep, there is a cutting off of the soul's activities in the body. This is close to the previous meaning.

It has also been said that **سُبات** means death, and the Glorified has counted sleep as death where He said: **وَهُوَ الَّذِي يَتَوَفَّاكُمْ بِاللَّيْلِ** — **"It is He who takes your souls by night"** (6:60). This is far-fetched. As for the verse, He, the Exalted, counted sleep as a "taking of the soul" (توفياً) but did not count it as death; rather, the Quran explicitly states the opposite: **اللهُ يَتَفَّى الْأَنْفُسَ حِينَ مَوْتِها وَالَّتِي لَمْ تَمُتْ فِي مَنامِها** — **"Allah takes the souls at the time of their death, and those who have not died, in their sleep."** (39:42).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَجَعَلْنَا اللَّيْلَ لِباساً** — **"and made the night a covering,"** (78:10). That is, a concealer that covers things with its darkness, which veils visible objects just as clothing covers the body. This is a divine cause that invites the cessation of movement and activity, and a leaning toward stillness, comfort, and returning to family and home.

It has been narrated from some that the night being a "covering" means it is like a garment for the day, making it easy to bring the day out from it—but this is as you see (i.e., weak).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَجَعَلْنَا النَّهارَ مَعاشاً** — **"and made the day for livelihood,"** (78:11). Livelihood (العيش) is life—as mentioned by al-Raghib—except that *'aysh* is specific to the life of animals; thus, one does not say the *'aysh* of Allah or the *'aysh* of the angels, but rather the life (حياة) of Allah and the life of the angels. The word **مَعاش** is a mim-infinitive (مصدر ميمي), a noun of time, or a noun of place. In the verse, it carries one of the latter two meanings. The sense is: We made the day a time for your life or a place for your life, wherein you seek the bounty of your Lord. It has also been said that it carries the infinitive meaning with a suppressed mudaf (مضاف), the estimation being: We made the day for the seeking of livelihood.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَبَنَيْنا فَوْقَكُمْ سَبْعاً شِداداً** — **"and built above you seven strong [heavens],"** (78:12). That is, seven heavens strong in their construction.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وجَعَلْنا سِراجاً وَهَّاجاً** — **"and made [the sun] a radiant lamp,"** (78:13). The word **وَهَّاج** (radiant) means intense in light and heat. What is meant by the radiant lamp is the sun.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَأَنْزَلْنا مِنَ الْمُعْصِراتِ ماءً ثَجَّاجاً** — **"and sent down from the rain-clouds pouring water,"** (78:14). The **مُعْصِرات** are the rain-bearing clouds. It has also been said they are the winds that squeeze the clouds to make them rain. The word **ثَجَّاج** means water that is poured out abundantly. Based on this latter meaning, it is better for **مِنَ** (from) to mean "by means of" (الباء).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **لِنُخْرِجَ بِهِ حَبًّا وَنَباتاً** — **"that We may bring forth with it grain and plants,"** (78:15). That is, grain and plants by which humans and other animals are nourished.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَجَنَّاتٍ أَلْفافاً** — **"and gardens of dense foliage."** (78:16). This is coordinated (معطوف) with His saying: **حَبًّا**. Gardens of dense foliage (جنات ألفاف) are those whose trees are intertwined with one another.

It has been said that **أَلْفاف** is a plural that has no singular form from its own root.

## (Narrative Discussion)

In some narrations: The "great tiding" (النبأ العظيم) is Ali (peace be upon him), and this is from the inner meaning (البطن).

In *al-Khisal*, from 'Ikrimah, from Ibn 'Abbas, who said: Abu Bakr said, "O Messenger of Allah, gray hair has come quickly upon you." He replied: **شيبتني هود والواقعة والمرسلات وعم يتساءلون** — "Surahs Hud, al-Waqi'ah, al-Mursalat, and 'Amma Yatasa'alun have made me gray."

In the *Tafsir* of al-Qummi, regarding His saying, the Exalted: **أَلَمْ نَجْعَلِ الْأَرْضَ مِهاداً**, he said: "Man finds rest (يمهد) in it." Regarding **وَالْجِبالَ أَوْتاداً**, he said: "Meaning the pegs of the earth."

In *Nahj al-Balaghah*, he (peace be upon him) said: **ووتد بالصخور ميدان أرضه** — "And He pegged with rocks the shaking of His earth."

In the *Tafsir* of al-Qummi, regarding His saying, the Exalted: **وَجَعَلْنَا اللَّيْلَ لِباساً**, he said: "It clothes the day."

**I say:** Perhaps what is meant is that it hides what the day manifests and covers what it discloses.

In the same source, regarding His saying, the Exalted: **وَجَعَلْنا سِراجاً وَهَّاجاً**, he said: "The illuminating sun." Regarding **وَأَنْزَلْنا مِنَ الْمُعْصِراتِ**, he said: "From the clouds." Regarding **ماءً ثَجَّاجاً**, he said: "Pouring upon pouring."

In the *Tafsir* of al-Ayyashi, from Abu 'Abdillah (peace be upon him) regarding the verse: **عامٌ فِيهِ يُغاثُ النَّاسُ وَفِيهِ يَعْصِرُونَ** — "a year in which the people will be helped and in which they will be rained upon (يعصرون)."

Then he said: "Have you not heard His saying: **وَأَنْزَلْنا مِنَ الْمُعْصِراتِ ماءً ثَجَّاجاً**?"

**I say:** What is meant is that **يَعْصِرُونَ** is read with a *damma* on the *ya* in the passive voice, meaning they are rained upon. His (peace be upon him) citation of the verse **وَأَنْزَلْنا مِنَ الْمُعْصِراتِ** is evidence that he (peace be upon him) took **الْمُعْصِرات** to mean "the rain-givers," from the expression "the cloud *a'sarat*" when it rains.

Al-Ayyashi narrated a similar hadith from Ali ibn Ma'mar, from his father, from Abu 'Abdillah (peace be upon him). Al-Qummi also narrated the like in his *Tafsir* from the Commander of the Faithful (peace be upon him).
